coredata.dc:title: Design of a Z-scheme g-C3N4/CQDs/CdIn2S4 composite for efficient visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of ibuprofen
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Mingxing
Zhang, Zhaosheng
Long, Run
Wang, Ying
Yu, Yajing
Pei, Yuansheng
coredata.dc:description:
A novel Z-scheme photocatalyst consisting of acidified graphitic carbon nitrogen (ag-C3N4)/carbon quantum dots/CdIn2S4 (CN/CQDs/CIS) was successfully synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method. The optimized CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS exhibited significantly improved photocatalytic performance in the degradation of ibuprofen under visible-light irradiation. Based on a series of characterizations, the ag-C3N4 and CQDs were distributed uniformly on the surface of the cubic spinel structure of CIS, with intimate contact among the materials. This intimate heterogeneous interface facilitated the migration of photogenerated carriers, further leading to enhanced photocatalytic performance. These results also indicated that the CQDs not only connect ag-C3N4 with CIS through covalent bonds but also enhance the visible-light adsorption. According to the analysis of the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) and Mott-Schottky curves, the mechanism of the Z-scheme heterojunction is proposed. The CQDs serve as electron mediators and transfer the electrons in the conduction band (CB) of ag-C3N4 to recombine with the holes in the valence band (VB) of CIS in the Z-scheme, leading to the enhanced separation efficiency of the photogenerated electrons in the CB of ag-C3N4 and the holes in the VB of CIS. The pollutant IBU was degraded by h
+ , ·O2 − and ·OH, as determined by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis.

Photocatalytic oxidation

head.ce:title: Design of a Z-scheme g-C3N4/CQDs/CdIn2S4 composite for efficient visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of ibuprofen

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A novel Z-scheme photocatalyst consisting of acidified graphitic carbon nitrogen (ag-C3N4)/carbon quantum dots/CdIn2S4 (CN/CQDs/CIS) was successfully synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method. The optimized CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS exhibited significantly improved photocatalytic performance in the degradation of ibuprofen under visible-light irradiation. Based on a series of characterizations, the ag-C3N4 and CQDs were distributed uniformly on the surface of the cubic spinel structure of CIS, with intimate contact among the materials. This intimate heterogeneous interface facilitated the migration of photogenerated carriers, further leading to enhanced photocatalytic performance. These results also indicated that the CQDs not only connect ag-C3N4 with CIS through covalent bonds but also enhance the visible-light adsorption. According to the analysis of the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) and Mott-Schottky curves, the mechanism of the Z-scheme heterojunction is proposed. The CQDs serve as electron mediators and transfer the electrons in the conduction band (CB) of ag-C3N4 to recombine with the holes in the valence band (VB) of CIS in the Z-scheme, leading to the enhanced separation efficiency of the photogenerated electrons in the CB of ag-C3N4 and the holes in the VB of CIS. The pollutant IBU was degraded by h
+ , ·O2 − and ·OH, as determined by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis.

The CQDs serve as a bridge to not only connect ag-C3N4 with CIS through covalent bonds but also transfer the electrons in the Z-scheme heterojunction, further leading to enhanced photocatalytic performance.
Image 1
This work further investigates the unique features of carbon quantum dots as an electron transfer mediator for fabrication of Z-scheme photocatalyst for degrading ibuprofen.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past two decades, pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs), which include medications, cosmetic ingredients, food supplements and personal hygiene products, have been cause for increasing concern worldwide due to their widespread distribution in water bodies and potential impacts on human health (Choina et al., 2013; Liang et al., 2015; Ort et al., 2010; Saeid et al., 2018). Many researchers have been devoted to developing efficient and economical methods for the removal of PPCPs from environmental matrices. In recent years, solar photocatalysis techniques have received considerable attention due to their facile utilization of the abundant natural sunlight, high mineralization efficiency, environmental friendliness and immense technological potential (Asahi et al., 2001; Di et al., 2015a; Kubacka et al., 2016). How to develop excellent photocatalysts that efficiently use clean and renewable sunlight energy has become a focal point of photocatalysis research.

To efficiently utilize the visible-light region (close to 43% of the solar spectrum) of sunlight, many visible-light-driven photocatalysts, such as BiVO4 (Ye et al., 2017), CaTiO3 (Humayun et al., 2016), BiOX (X = Br, Cl) (Di et al., 2015b; Long et al., 2015), WO3/graphitic carbon nitrogen (g-C3N4) (Xiao et al., 2018), Ag3PO4 (Chen et al., 2017), and the ternary sulfides of AB2X4 chalcogenides (Chen et al., 2015a; Saidaminov et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017), have been studied extensively. The ternary sulfides of AB2X4 chalcogenides (A = Cd2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+ etc.; BMn3+, Fe3+, Al3+, In3+ etc.; X = O2−, S2−, Se2−, Te2− etc.) are distinguished from other visible-light-active photocatalysts because of their appropriate band gaps, suitable band edges, superior photoelectrochemical properties and high recycling rate (Yang et al., 2016). As a notable member of the AB2X4 family of semiconductors, CdIn2S4 (CIS), with a narrow band gap (approximately 2.1 eV), has a strong light-harvesting ability, tunable reactive sites, and unique optoelectronic and structural properties (Carević et al., 2015; Xia et al., 2016b). Numerous investigations have indicated that CIS has high photocatalytic activity in hydrogen generation (Xue et al., 2017), organic pollutant degradation (Chen et al., 2016a) and CO2 reduction (Liu et al., 2016). However, the photocatalytic efficiency of pure CIS is restricted by the high recombination of photoinduced charge carriers, the shortcomings of photocorrosion under light irradiation and the presence of Cd2+ dissolved in solution. A great deal of research, including studies on element doping and heterojunction construction by coupling with other semiconductors, has been devoted to solving the problems above (Li et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017). Coupling CIS with g-C3N4 via the construction of a type II heterojunction has been reported in several studies (Chen et al., 2016a; Liu et al., 2016). However, the charge-separation efficiency is not high, and this type II heterojunction is unable to maximize the redox ability of the holes and electrons. The Fermi levels of the semiconductors and electron mediators have not been considered in mechanistic interpretations.

For Z-scheme heterojunction construction, it is essential to seek appropriate electron mediators between CIS and g-C3N4. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a well-known nanosized carbon material with at least one dimension less than 10 nm in size, have increasingly been applied in photocatalysis due to their facile synthesis, low cost, nontoxicity and excellent photoelectrochemical properties (Yu et al., 2016). CQDs composed of sp2 (graphitic carbon) and sp3 (diamond-like) hybridized carbon atoms have various surface functional groups, such as carbonyl, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. The emission of sp2 clusters insulated by sp3 networks leads to the unique CQD property of intense photoluminescence (PL) (Chien et al., 2012; Loh et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013). In addition, CQDs can act as both electron acceptors and electron donors (Fu et al., 2018), which indicates that CQDs could accelerate electron transfer and subsequently reduce the recombination of photoinduced carriers in CQD-based photocatalysts under irradiation. CQDs have been integrated with many different semiconductors to produce hybrid photocatalysts with improved performance, such as CQDs/Bi2WO6 (Di et al., 2015a), CQDs/BiOX (X = Br, Cl) (Qu et al., 2018; Xia et al., 2016a), CQDs/BiVO4 (Nan et al., 2015), CQDs/molecular nickel (Martindale et al., 2015) and CQDs/CoMn-S (Fu et al., 2018).

In this work, a novel Z-scheme photocatalyst that combines CIS with ag-C3N4 and uses CQDs as a bridge to serve as an electron transfer mediator was developed by hydrothermal method. CQDs have abundant functional groups on their surface and low Fermi levels, which enable them to connect CIS to ag-C3N4 via covalent bonds and facilitate efficiently electron transfer. The structure, morphology, components, and optical and electrochemical properties of the as-prepared semiconductors were systematically investigated. IBU, a typical propionic acid compound in the nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) class, was chosen as a typical PPCPs pollutant. The photocatalytic degradation of IBU by the developed photocatalyst, the associated degradation mechanism, electron transfer and conversion pathways were analyzed thoroughly.

All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The ibuprofen sodium salt (≥98%) was used as a target pollutant duo to the low solubility of pure ibuprofen. A dialysis bag (wide flat: 44 MM, MWCO: 500 Da) was obtained from MYM Biological Technology Company Limited (American). Millipore water (18.2 MΩ cm @ 25 °C) was used in all synthetic and analytical procedures.

The synthetic methods of water-soluble CQDs and acidified g-C3N4 (ag-C3N4) were presented in the supplementary material. All composites were prepared by the hydrothermal method. First, cadmium chloride (CdCl2·2.5H2O, 1.5 mmol), indium nitrate hydrate (InN3O9·xH2O, 3 mmol) and thioacetamide (C2H5NS, 6 mmol) were dissolved in 105 mL of ultrapure water. After stirring for 15 min, CQDs and ag-C3N4 were added into the above solution. After additional stirring and ultrasonication for 1 h, the mixture was transferred into a 150 mL container in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature, and the obtained precipitates were then centrifuged and washed with absolute ethanol and ultrapure water several times. The final products were dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

The CQDs/CIS composites with different amounts of added CQDs (0.00 g, 0.025 g, 0.05 g, 0.075 g, 0.1 g, 0.15 g and 0.2 g) were denoted CIS, CQDs-1/CIS, CQDs-2/CIS, CQDs-3/CIS, CQDs-4/CIS, CQDs-5/CIS and CQDs-6/CIS, respectively. The optimal CQDs addition was 0.075 g. The ag-C3N4/CQDs/CIS ternary composites with different amounts of added ag-C3N4 (0.0705 g, 0.2115 g, 0.3525 g, 0.4935 g, and 0.705 g) were denoted CN-1/CQDs-3/CIS, CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS, CN-3/CQDs-3/CIS, CN-4/CQDs-3/CIS and CN-5/CQDs-3/CIS, respectively.

The crystalline phase was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Malvern PANalytical, X’pert Pro MPD) with Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the samples were acquired using a Nexus 670 (Osic Holding) spectrophotometer over a scan range of 400–4000 cm−1. The Raman spectra were collected using a LabRAM Aramis spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ThermoFisher, ESCALAB 250Xi) with Al Kα radiation was conducted to analyze elements and valence states. The morphology of the photocatalysts were investigated by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, S-4800/EX-350) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100F). The light adsorption capacity of the photocatalysts was determined by the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS, Varian, Cary 5000). Active radicals in the photocatalytic reaction were identified by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, Bruker, E 500). The photoelectrochemical performance was measured by a PGSTAT204 electrochemical workstation (Metrohm Autolab B.V., made in The Netherlands) in a typical three-electrode system. The intermediates were determined by HPLC-MS technique (ACQUITY UPLC/Xevo QTOF-MS/MS).

The photocatalytic degradation experiments were performed in a thermostatic water bath maintained at 20 °C. A 300 W xenon lamp equipped with a 420 nm cutoff filter was employed as the visible-light source, of which the illumination intensity was maintained ca. 200 mW/cm2. In each experiment, 0.1 g of the photocatalyst was suspended in 100 mL of an 80 mg/L IBU aqueous solution under magnetic stirring. Prior to irradiation, the mixture was magnetically stirred for 1 h in the dark to establish the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. During illumination, 2 mL of the reactant suspension was withdrawn at each 15 min interval and subsequently filtered by using a 0.22 μm PES filter membrane to remove the photocatalyst. The residual IBU concentration in the solution was determined by an LC-20AT high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) system (Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with a C-18 column (Inertsil ODS-3, 250 mm × 4.6 mm i.d., 5 μm). The mineralization efficiency of the final reaction solution was analyzed by a total organic carbon (TOC) analyzer (TOC-L CPN, Shimadzu, Japan).

The density functional theory calculations are performed using the plane-wave pseudopotential methods as implemented within the Vienna Ab Initio Simulation Package (Kresse, 1996; Kresse and Furthmüller, 1996). The generalized gradient approximation Perdew–Burke–Ernserhof functional is used to describe electron exchange and correlation (Perdew et al., 1996), and the projector augmented-wave approach is employed to treat the electron-ion interaction (Blöchl, 1994). The plane wave energy cutoff is set to 400 eV to converge the total energy. Geometry optimization stops until the forces on ions are less than 10−3 eV Å−1 using 2 × 2 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack k-point mesh (Monkhorst and Pack, 1976). A much denser 6 × 6 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack k-point mesh was used for electronic properties calculations (Monkhorst and Pack, 1976). The long-range van der Waals interactions are described using the Grimme DFT-D3 method (Grimme, 2006; Grimme et al., 2010; Grimme et al., 2011).

To model the CN/CIS system, a 56-atom CN sheet interfacing with a 112-atom CIS (111) surface forms a periodic slab along the ab plane. For the CN/CQD/CIS, a 30-atom CQD was put on the CIS (111) surface via C-In covalent bonds binding the CIS and CN. A 15 Å vacuum layers separate 168-atom CN/CIS and 198-atom CN/CQD/CIS supercell replicas along z-axis to eliminate the interaction between images.

Powder XRD was conducted to identify the crystallographic structure and phase purity of the samples. As shown in Fig. 1 a, all diffraction peaks of the CIS sample can be exactly assigned to the cubic spinel structure of CIS (JCPDS 27-0060). The peaks at 2θ values of 14.2°, 23.2°, 27.3°, 28.5°, 33.1°, 40.8°, 43.4°, 47.4°, 55.6°, 59.0°, 66.2°, and 76.7° correspond to the (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), (440), (533), (444), (731) and (662) planes of cubic spinel CIS, respectively (Apte et al., 2010; Kale et al., 2006). No other impurity peaks, such as CdS or In2S3, were observed, indicating the high purity of the as-prepared sample. The pattern of pristine ag-C3N4 has an intense diffraction peak located at 27.4° and a weak peak at 13.3°, which can be indexed to the (002) and (100) diffraction planes of g-C3N4 (JCPDS 87-1526), representing the interplanar stacking peak of conjugated aromatic systems and the in-plane structural packing of tris-triazine units, respectively (Du et al., 2015; Han et al., 2015). In addition, the XRD spectrum of CQDs shows a broad diffraction peak at 30.2° 2θ, which corresponds to a lattice spacing of 3 Å, similar to the (002) reflection (d
002 = 3.4 Å) of disordered graphitic-like species (Peng and Travassejdic, 2009; Zhou et al., 2007). The low signal-to-noise ratio is consistent with a significant portion of the sample being amorphous (Fig. S1.) (Martindale et al., 2015). For CQDs/CIS and CN/CQDs/CIS composites with different mass ratios, the XRD pattern is not obviously different from that of pure CIS (Fig. S1.), demonstrating that the introduction of CQDs or ag-C3N4 nanosheets into the reaction does not influence the formation and crystalline phase of cubic spinel CIS. Surprisingly, no typical crystalline phase of ag-C3N4 appeared in any of the composites, which could be ascribed to the following reasons: (i) the diffraction peak of the (100) plane cannot be observed in any of the composites because the regular stack of ag-C3N4 was destroyed by the intercalation of CIS and/or because of the low XRD intensity of ag-C3N4; (ii) the position of the diffraction peaks for ag-C3N4 (27.4°) and CIS (27.3°) are too close, resulting in the diffraction overlap of the ag-C3N4 (002) and CIS (311) planes. To confirm the existence of ag-C3N4 and CQDs in the composites, FT-IR spectra and Raman spectra were examined.

The existence of CQDs in the samples can be demonstrated by Raman (Fig. 1b) and FT-IR (Fig. 1c) spectroscopy. The Raman spectrum of the CIS sample contains a typical vibrational peak at 304 cm−1, which is assigned to the Raman-forbidden F1u (TO) modes of the CIS stretching mode (Unger et al., 2010). The broad peaks centered at 3280 and 2920 cm−1 appeared after the CQDs were coupled with CIS and CN/CIS, respectively. For the CQDs/CIS and CN/CQDs/CIS composites, the intensity of the CIS peak decreased as the peak corresponding to the CQDs appeared, corroborating the successful incorporation of CQDs in the CQDs/CIS and CN/CQDs/CIS composites. Moreover, the IR spectrum of the CQDs shows a broad adsorption peak centered at 3418 cm−1 and two strong features at ν = 1391, 1568 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of O-H and the symmetric and antisymmetric stretches of the carboxylate group, respectively. CQDs have many oxygen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, on their surface. With regard to the CQDs/CIS composite, weak peaks at 3418, 1391 and 1586 cm−1 are observed, which represent the existence of CQDs in the CQDs/CIS sample. As shown in Fig. 1c, the FT-IR spectrum of pristine ag-C3N4 presents a broad band centered at 3156 cm−1 originating from the typical O-H stretching vibration of the surface-adsorbed water molecules and the N-H stretching vibration modes of either residual NH2 groups attached to the sp2-hybridized carbon or NH groups located at the defect sites on the aromatic ring (Jo and Clament Sagaya Selvam, 2015; Tay et al., 2015). The peak at 1632 cm−1 is associated with CN stretching vibration modes (Li et al., 2014; Zang et al., 2014). The other prominent absorption peaks located at 1547, 1456, 1406, 1320, and 1236 cm−1 are attributed to the characteristic stretching vibration of C-N heterocycles (Li et al., 2014; Zang et al., 2014). Moreover, the sharp peak at 804 cm−1 is due to the bending vibration of the s-triazine ring units (Li et al., 2014; Zang et al., 2014). After coupling ag-C3N4 with the CQDs/CIS composites, the resulting composite exhibited the same featured adsorption peaks as ag-C3N4, indicating the successful incorporation of ag-C3N4 into the CQDs/CIS composite.

The morphology and structure of the as-obtained samples were observed by SEM, TEM and HRTEM techniques (Fig. 2 ). Fig. 2a shows that pure CIS has a microspherical morphology with a diameter of approximately 6 μm and self-assembled into numerous cubes. After introduction of the CQDs, the surface of the microspheres with a decreased diameter of 4 μm consisted of abundant nanosheets (Liu et al., 2016). When coupled with ag-C3N4, the ag-C3N4 nanosheets covered the surface of the CQDs/CIS, as shown in Fig. 2c. The cubic spinel structure of CIS is observed in the TEM image of CQDs/CIS in Fig. 2d, which is in good agreement with the XRD analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 2e, the CQDs are spherical nanoparticles with diameters of 4–7 nm. The characteristic lattice spacings of CIS corresponding to (311) and (220) facets were labelled in Fig. 2f. Fig. 2g reveals the lattice spacing of the CQDs (0.218 nm) and CIS (0.326, 0.383 and 0.613 nm), which correspond to reflection over the (100) lattice plane of CQDs (Baker and Baker, 2010; Ghosh et al., 2014) and the (311), (220) and (111) planes of CIS (Chen et al., 2016a; Liu et al., 2016), respectively. As revealed by the elemental mappings, the Cd, In, S, C and N elements are homogeneously distributed throughout the samples, confirming that CQDs, ag-C3N4 are loaded on CIS successfully (Fig. S2). Moreover, CQDs and ag-C3N4 are distributed uniformly on the surface of CIS with intimate contact among the materials, as observed in Fig. 2h. The intimate heterogeneous interface will facilitate the migration of photogenerated carriers, further leading to enhanced photocatalytic performance.

The surface chemical compositions of the as-prepared samples and the internal interaction between CQDs, ag-C3N4 and CIS were investigated by XPS. The survey XPS spectrum in Fig. 3 a indicates the existence of O in CQDs and C and N in the CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS composite. Fig. 3b shows the fine scan XPS spectra in the C 1s regions. For CIS, only one peak is observed at 284.8 eV, which belongs to the reference carbon. The C 1s spectrum of the CQDs shows two strong peaks at 284.8 and 281.8 eV, which correspond to the C-C and CO environments, respectively (Deng et al., 2014). The C 1s region of ag-C3N4 could be deconvoluted into two peaks at 284.8 and 288.3 eV, which are related to adventitious carbon and sp2-bonded carbon (-NC-N). For the CQDs-3/CIS composite, the C 1s region could be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.8, 285.3, 286.2 and 288.3 eV, which correspond to C-C, C-S, C-O and CO environments, respectively (Chen et al., 2016a; Fu et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2016). This result demonstrates the existence of covalent bonds connecting CQDs with CIS in the composite. The C 1s spectrum of CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS also presents four peaks at 284.4, 285.0, 285.6 and 288.1 eV that are slightly shifted toward lower binding energies. The N 1s curve of ag-C3N4 (Fig. 3c) can be divided into three peaks centered at 398.7, 399.3 and 400.9 eV, corresponding to the nitrogen atoms in the C-NC and N-(C)3 groups and N-H bonds, respectively (Chen et al., 2016b; Chen et al., 2015b). Compared with that of pristine ag-C3N4, the N 1s spectrum of the coupled CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS composites also possesses three peaks with a weak shift. Similar phenomena are observed in the Cd 3d, In 3d and S 2p regions. The Cd 3d, In 3d and S 2p XPS spectra of the as-prepared samples are shown in Fig. 3d–f. The main binding energies of pure CIS located at 404.8, 411.5, 444.25, 451.75, 161.1 and 162.3 eV are in concordance with Cd 3d5/2, Cd3d3/2, In 3d5/2, In 3d3/2, S 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively, demonstrating that the valence states of Cd, In and S are +2, +3 and −2, respectively (Fan and Guo, 2008). The shift in the binding energy of the as-prepared composites indicates the chemical bond interactions among CIS, CQDs and ag-C3N4.

To study the optical properties of the photocatalysts, UV/Vis DRS were collected for the samples. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, pure CIS and ag-C3N4 showed strong visible-light absorption with absorption edges at approximately 680 nm and 600 nm, respectively. The CQDs/CIS composites exhibited significantly increased visible-light absorption as the initial weight ratio of CQDs increased (Fig. S3), demonstrating that CQDs have excellent visible-light absorption properties and thus can utilize more solar light than pure CIS. However, the absorption intensity of the CN/CQDs/CIS samples in the visible-light region is weaker than that of the CQDs/CIS composites (Fig. S3), which may be attributed to the shielding effect of ag-C3N4 (Chen et al., 2016a; Liu et al., 2016): the ag-C3N4 nanosheets wrapped around the surface of CIS microspheres and largely blocked the absorption of incident light.

Based on the UV/vis DRS, the band gap of a semiconductor can be determined by the Kubelka-Munk function. As shown in Fig. 4b, according to the x-axis intercept of the linear portion of the plot, the band gaps of pure CIS, ag-C3N4, CQDs-3/CIS and CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS were estimated to be 2.29, 2.67, 2.27 and 2.26 eV, respectively.

The photoabsorption capability of the catalysts affects the photocatalytic activity, but other factors, such as the separation efficiency of photoinduced electron-hole pairs and charge transfer resistance, are also vital for evaluating the photocatalytic performance. The transient photocurrent responses (i-t curves) with or without visible-light irradiation and PL spectroscopy were investigated to estimate the recombination of electron-hole pairs. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the photocurrent densities of the CQDs-3/CIS and CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS samples were higher than that of pure CIS. This result could be ascribed to the rapid transfer of photogenerated electrons by CQDs. Additionally, it demonstrated that the efficiency in separating and migrating photoinduced charge carriers was dramatically improved after introduction of CQDs and ag-C3N4. The results are consistent with the PL spectra analysis (Fig. 5b). The strong emissive peak of pure CIS located at 562 nm resulted from the recombination of photogenerated carriers. After introduction of the CQDs, the intensity of the emission peak was lower than that of pristine CIS. Furthermore, the CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS composite exhibited negligible PL emission compared with pure CIS. This result implies that CQDs and ag-C3N4 could remarkably enhance the separation efficiency of photoinduced electron-hole pairs.

EIS measurements (Fig. 5c) were also conducted to evaluate the interfacial charge transfer resistance. The arc radius of pristine CIS, CQDs-3/CIS and CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS exhibited a gradual decreasing trend. The arc radius in the EIS Nyquist plot of CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS was overwhelmingly smaller than that of pure CIS, indicating that CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS possessed decreased charge transfer resistance, thus leading to a higher mobility of photoinduced charge carriers and photocatalytic performance.

To estimate the band structure of the semiconductors, Mott-Schottky measurements were carried out. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, the positive slopes of the fitted lines indicate that all samples are n-type semiconductors. The flat band potential (Vfb) can be obtained from the x-intercept of the linear region in the Mott-Schottky plot. The Vfb values of ag-C3N4, pure CIS, CQDs-3/CIS and CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS were determined to be −1.24, −0.60, −0.47 and −0.68 V (vs. SCE), corresponding to −1.00, −0.36, −0.23 and −0.44 V (vs. NHE), respectively. Reportedly, Vfb is approximately equal to the Fermi level (Ef) for n-type semiconductors (Ding et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2018). Hence, the Ef values of ag-C3N4, CIS, CQDs-3/CIS and CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS are −1.00, −0.36, −0.23 and −0.44 eV (vs. NHE), respectively. Accordingly, the conduction band (CB) of ag-C3N4, CIS, CQDs-3/CIS and CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS could be calculated as −1.20, −0.56, −0.43 and −0.64 eV, respectively. Based on the band gap determined from analysis of the UV–vis DRS, the valence band (VB) position of ag-C3N4, CIS, CQDs-3/CIS and CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS could be calculated as 1.47, 1.73 1.84 and 1.62 eV, respectively, by the following empirical formula: EVB = ECB + Eg. The diagrams of the energy band structure over ag-C3N4, CIS, CQDs-3/CIS and CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS are schematically depicted in Fig. 5e. The Fermi level of CQDs-3/CIS was more positive than that of pristine CIS because the CQDs possessed a Fermi level of Ef = −0.08 eV, which was more positive than that of pure CIS. Therefore, upon the intimate contact formation between the CQDs and CIS, a Schottky barrier to electron migration from CIS to the CQDs formed. Generally, the accumulation of electrons inevitably causes the Fermi level to shift. As a result, the Fermi level of CIS shifted down to a more positive potential until equilibrium was established, which resulted in a shorter migration pathway for photoinduced electrons. Analogously, electrons were transferred from ag-C3N4 to CQDs-3/CIS due to the more positive Fermi level of ag-C3N4. When balance was achieved, the Fermi level of CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS shifted from −0.23 to −0.44 eV.

The photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared samples was evaluated by degrading IBU under visible-light irradiation. As illustrated in Fig. 6 , the concentration of IBU did not obviously change under 60 min of visible-light irradiation without the addition of photocatalyst, indicating that the photolysis of IBU can be ignored. The introduction of different contents of CQDs could clearly enhance the photocatalytic activity of the hybrid photocatalysts, which may be attributed to the improvement in the visible-light absorption properties and reduction in photoinduced carrier recombination. Furthermore, the IBU degradation efficiency firstly increased with increasing CQDs loading contents and then decreasing. Similarly, after coupling with different loading contents of ag-C3N4, CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS exhibited highest photocatalytic activity (91.0% removal of IBU). However, with a further increase in the coupled content of ag-C3N4, the photocatalytic performance of the composites decreased. The ag-C3N4 wrapped over the surface of CIS blocked the adsorption of incident light. The TOC removal efficiency (inset in Fig. 6) of CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS was 85.0%, which was 48.6% higher than pure CIS.

In order to rationalize the enhanced photocatalytic activity upon introducing CQDs into the hybrid photocatalysts, we calculated the electronic properties of the CN/CIS and CN/CQD/CIS system by means of DFT calculations. Shown in part a and b of Fig. 7 , the total charge density plots demonstrate that inserting CQD between CN and CIS facilitates interfacial charge separation because CQD increases the CN-CIS interaction, verifying by the fact that charge accumulates on the interfacial regime, particularly localizing on CQD. The planar charge-density projections (Fig. 7c and d) confirms this observation. In addition, the differential charge density can directly reflect the charge redistribution in the CIS/CN system with and without inserting CQD. A comparison between Fig. 7e and f demonstrates that the CQD provides charge transfer channel between CN and CIS, enhancing the charge extraction from the CN to the CIS and reducing the nonradiative charge recombination. As a result, the excited-state lifetime is extended and the photocatalytic activity is improved.

Part a and b of Fig. 8 shows the calculated planar average electrostatic potential of the CIS/CN and CIS/CQD/CN. Because electrons are transferred from the CN to the CIS, which leads to formation a built-in electric field at the CIS-CN interface. The value of the built-in electric is estimated to be the difference of the planar average electrostatic potential of the two species. Upon CQD introduction, the built-in electric field increases to 2.1 eV in the CIS/CQD/CN from 0.86 eV in the CIS/CN, favoring electron transfer from the CN to the CIS in the former case and rationalizing further the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the CIS/CN system containing with CQD.

To reveal the mechanism of pollutant degradation, EPR spin-trapping with the DMPO technique was conducted to determine the active species over pristine CIS and CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS composite in the photocatalytic reaction. As shown in Fig. 9 a, four characteristic peaks of DMPO-·OH are present under visible-light irradiation, while weak peaks are observed under dark conditions. Moreover, stronger peak intensities were observed for the CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS composite than for pure CIS, which indicated that the CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS composite could generate more ·OH to oxidize IBU. Notably, this phenomenon was also observed in the signals of DMPO-·O2 − (Fig. 9b). Hence, the EPR analysis demonstrates the generation of ·OH and ·O2 − in the photocatalytic process. Compared with pure CIS, the CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS composite could produce more ·OH and ·O2 −, which might have originated from the higher separation efficiency of photoinduced electron-hole pairs in the Z-scheme.

As mentioned above, CQDs play a crucial role in the photocatalytic process. The experiments on the photocatalytic reduction of AgNO3 (Fig. S4) demonstrates that the CQDs possess unique rapid electron transfer properties that facilitate charge separation and increase the concentration of charge carriers available for photoreaction. Based on the characterization, the proposed degradation mechanism of the CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS composite is believed to follow a Z-scheme mechanism, as shown in Fig. 9c. According to the diagram of energy band positions, upon generation of intimate contact between CIS and ag-C3N4, electrons are transferred from the semiconductor with a high Fermi level to that with a low Fermi level until their Ef values remain at the same level (Huang et al., 2017). Namely, the electrons are transferred from ag-C3N4 to CIS, which results in the formation of a built-in electric field at the interface between ag-C3N4 and CIS. The interfacial electric field then causes the depletion or accumulation of free charge carriers near the semiconductor surface. Ultimately, the energy band edges of ag-C3N4 with a high Fermi level bend upward toward the interface, while those of CIS with a low Fermi level bend downward toward the interface. Notably, the downward-bending band allows electrons to flow out freely while inhibiting the hole outflow. In contrast, the upward-bending band enables the holes but not electrons to migrate. Therefore, when irradiated under visible light, the CIS and ag-C3N4 are photoexcited to generate electrons (e
- ) and holes (h
+ ), and the electrons migrate from the VB to CB. First, the photogenerated electrons in the CB of CIS are transferred to the CQDs due to the lower Fermi level of the CQDs (Ef = −0.08 eV) and the downward-bending CB of CIS. Meanwhile, the electrons in the CB of ag-C3N4 could not be transferred to the CQDs because of the upward-bending CB of ag-C3N4. CQDs, as an excellent electronic transmission mediator, transfer the as-obtained electrons to recombine with the holes in the VB of ag-C3N4, subsequently leading to enhanced separation efficiency of the photogenerated electrons in the CB of ag-C3N4 and holes in the VB of CIS. Compared with that of CIS, the CB of ag-C3N4 is more negative, which indicates that the electrons in the CB of ag-C3N4 have higher reactivity with O2. Similarly, the holes in the VB of CIS have higher reactivity with organic pollutants than that of ag-C3N4. Furthermore, the electrons remaining in the CB of ag-C3N4 (−1.20 eV vs. NHE) could reduce O2 to ·O2 − since its CB potential is more negative than E0 (O2/·O2 −) (−0.046 eV vs. NHE). However, hydroxyl radicals do not originate from the reaction between the holes and OH− because the holes remaining in the VB of CIS (1.73 eV vs. NHE) are more negative than E0 (OH−/·OH) (2.31 eV vs. NHE). However, the EPR experiment demonstrated the existence of ·OH, indicating that ·OH might originate from the reaction between superoxide radicals and other species. The possible reactions in the photocatalytic process are as follows:
(1)
(2)
(3)
(4)

An ag-C3N4/CQDs/CIS composite that behaves as a Z-scheme photocatalyst was successfully developed via hydrothermal method. Based on a series of characterizations, the ag-C3N4 and CQDs were distributed uniformly on the surface of the cubic spinel structure of CIS with intimate contact among the materials. The introduction of CQDs enhanced the ability to adsorb visible light. The CQDs and ag-C3N4 increased the efficiency of the separation and migration of photoinduced charge carriers, and the optimized composite (CN-2/CQDs-3/CIS) exhibited significantly improved photocatalytic performance in the degradation of IBU under visible-light irradiation. The EPR results indicated that ·OH and ·O2 − were generated during the photocatalytic process, which mineralized IBU into CO2 and H2O. According to the analysis of characterizations, the Z-scheme mechanism was proposed. The CQDs serve as a bridge to not only connect ag-C3N4 with CIS through covalent bonds but also transfer the electrons in the CB of CIS to recombine with the holes in the VB of ag-C3N4 in the Z-scheme, further leading to enhanced separation efficiency of the photogenerated electrons in the CB of ag-C3N4 and the holes in the VB of CIS. Therefore, the Z-scheme heterostructure was responsible for the improved photocatalytic activity. This study provides a novel strategy for exploiting CQDs-based, visible-light-responsive, Z-scheme photocatalysts, which are utilized for environmental remediation.
